tldr: Cloudflare is a broad CDN, DNS, security, and edge-compute platform, while Vercel is a frontend cloud optimized for Next.js with managed CDN, edge, and serverless functions. Migration is most natural for teams whose primary use of Cloudflare was Pages + Workers and who already standardize on Next.js.
why_switch: Best-in-class Next.js deployment and preview UX
Tight integration with Git provider workflows
Less configuration for typical frontend apps
Native support for ISR, image optimization, analytics
what_to_export: Pages project source repos and build settings
KV / D1 / R2 data referenced from Workers
export_method: Cloudflare Pages source code is already in Git, which is the main export. From the dashboard or API capture build command, output directory, environment variables, deploy hooks, and custom domains. DNS records can be exported as a zone file or via Terraform's Cloudflare provider. Worker source lives in your wrangler project; document each Worker, its routes, and its bindings (KV, D1, R2, Queues). For data in KV, D1, or R2, export with the appropriate API: KV pairs via the API, D1 via SQL dump, R2 via S3-compatible tooling. TLS managed by Cloudflare cannot be exported; Vercel will issue its own. Confirm exact API endpoints and CLI commands against current Cloudflare documentation.
import_method: Connect the same Git repository in Vercel, choose the framework preset (typically Next.js), and configure environment variables in Project Settings, scoped per environment. Translate _redirects and _headers into Next.js or Vercel configuration (next.config.js, vercel.json) as appropriate. Re-implement Workers and Pages Functions as Vercel Serverless or Edge Functions, taking into account runtime differences and execution limits. KV, D1, and R2 do not have native Vercel replacements; either keep them on Cloudflare and call them remotely, or migrate to a third-party data service supported by Vercel integrations. Move custom domains in Vercel, validate TLS, and switch DNS gradually with lowered TTLs. Verify exact Vercel project settings and function APIs against current Vercel documentation.

concept_mapping.target: External service or kept on Cloudflare

effort: 1-4 weeks for typical Pages-only sites; more if Workers, KV, D1, or R2 are in critical path
gotchas: Cloudflare Workers (V8 isolates) and Vercel Edge Functions have similar but not identical APIs and limits
KV, D1, and R2 have no native Vercel equivalents; plan a separate data strategy
Vercel pricing can spike with bandwidth and serverless invocations; model expected costs before cutover
Some Cloudflare-only features (Access, Bot Management, full DNS proxying) have no direct Vercel equivalent
TLS certs from Cloudflare cannot be exported; Vercel provisions its own
ISR, image optimization, and analytics are first-class on Vercel but require config to truly replace Cloudflare equivalents

email.subject: Winding down our Cloudflare hosting over the next 14 days
email.body:
Hi Cloudflare team,

We are moving our frontend hosting from Cloudflare Pages to Vercel over the next 14 days, mainly because most of our work is now Next.js and we want the tightest fit with that framework.

In this window we plan to run Vercel deploys in parallel with Cloudflare Pages, then cut over custom domains with lowered DNS TTLs. Workers and Pages Functions will be re-implemented as Vercel Serverless / Edge Functions, and any KV, D1, or R2 data we depend on will either stay on Cloudflare or move to an external service depending on the workload.

Could you confirm how long build history, analytics, and Pages deployments remain accessible after we stop new deploys, plus the cleanest order to disable Workers, KV, D1, and R2 to avoid surprise charges or data loss?

Thanks for the support.

Your customer
